prs.title: [Perf] Re-enable dual-stream input projection for Qwen3/Qwen3.5 GDN
prs.author: jhsmith409
prs.body_head:
# Re-enable dual-stream execution of input projection for Qwen3/Qwen3.5 GDN

## Purpose

Restores the parallel execution of `in_proj_qkvz` and `in_proj_ba` that was
introduced in #36795 and reverted in #38152. The revert was necessary because
the original `gdn_in_proj` custom op took the layer name as a string, which
caused torch.compile to produce one compiled artifact per GDN layer (~4×
cold-compile regression, see #38152's description).

#38123 added the `LayerName`/`OpaqueObject` mechanism precisely so custom ops
can accept layer-name arguments without baking them into the graph. This PR
rewires the `gdn_in_proj` custom op to use `LayerName` via the existing
`_encode_layer_name` / `_resolve_layer_name` helpers — matching the pattern
already in use by `mamba_mixer2`, `gdn_attention_core`, and other mamba ops
that were updated in #38123.

The serial fallback path is preserved when `aux_stream()` returns `None`
(non-CUDA platforms); `forward_xpu` is unchanged.

## Why this is not duplicating an existing PR

Checked on 2026-04-13:

```
gh pr list --repo vllm-project/vllm --state open \
  --search "gdn_in_proj OR 'dual stream' qwen"
```

No open PR re-enables dual-stream for Qwen3/Qwen3.5. #38123's TODO in #38152
is still outstanding.

## Test plan

Tested on a workstation with an RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Max-Q (SM 12.0, 97 GB),
`vllm/vllm-openai:cu130-nightly` image (vllm
`0.19.1rc1.dev231+g9dd5ee011`), against `cyankiwi/Qwen3.5-35B-A3B-AWQ-4bit`
with `--quantization compressed-tensors --kv-cache-dtype fp8
--enable-prefix-caching --max-num-seqs 8 --gpu-memory-utilization 0.5`.

Three server configurations, **GPU held constant**, only image/overlay
changing:

| Config | Seq decode | Par 2 combined | Par 4 combined | Par 8 combined |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `cu130-nightly-mar23` (pre-#38152 regression) | 198.3 | 342.3 | 653.9 | 1119.1 |
| `cu130-nightly` (current, #38152 in effect) | 183.5 | 298.7 | 582.0 | 1030.8 |
| `cu130-nightly` + this PR | **197.9** | **342.2** |

prs.title: [WIP] Add TRITON_MLA_SPARSE backend for SM80 sparse MLA support
prs.author: haosdent
prs.body_head:
## Purpose

Closes #38006. Enables sparse MLA models (GLM-5, DeepSeek-V3.2) on SM80 (A100/A800) and SM121 (GB10/DGX Spark), where DeepGEMM / FlashMLA-Sparse / FlashInfer-MLA-Sparse are unavailable.

## Changes

1. **Dispatch guard.** `has_deep_gemm()` → `is_deep_gemm_supported()` (SM90+ arch check) in `sparse_attn_indexer.py` and `indexer.py`. Stops DeepGEMM SM90+ kernels from being invoked on SM80/SM121 when the package is installed.
2. **Triton `fp8_mqa_logits` for the indexer.** `mqa_logits_triton.py` reproduces DeepGEMM's prefill + paged MQA logits with a uint8/int bit-trick (`_decode_e4m3fn`) so the kernel compiles on SM80 without `tl.float8e4nv`. Matches the torch reference to bf16 ULPs.
3. **`TRITON_MLA_SPARSE` backend.** `triton_sparse_mla_kernel.py` adds a split-KV decode path with an N-way online-softmax merge, plus a single-pass fast path. Autotune over `(num_warps, num_stages) × (index_topk, NUM_KV_SPLITS, kv_group_num)`; warmed at backend init so no sweep cost in the first request. Masked-out sentinel is `-1e30` (not `-inf`) to avoid `NaN` from `(-inf) − (-inf)` when a whole BLOCK_N tile is masked on short prefills.
4. **Cudagraph support.** `TritonMLASparseMetadataBuilder` (new subclass of the XPU builder) advertises `AttentionCGSupport.UNIFORM_BATCH`; XPU's own builder stays at `NEVER`. On A100 TP=8 this flipped `FULL_AND_PIECEWISE` back on and collapsed the per-layer Python-loop overhead.

Layer 1 of #38006 (the C++ `dsv3_fused_a_gemm` kernel) is already handled upstream — `CMakeLists.txt` builds it for SM90+ only, with a `torch.nn.functional.linear` fallback.

## Benchmarks

Kernel (A30, SM80, 56 SMs):

| shape (nt, topk, H) | XPU kernel | this PR | speedup |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1, 2048, 128 (decode) | 0.297 ms | 0.094 ms | **3.15×** |
| 1, 4096, 128 | 0.580 ms | 0.167 ms | 3.46× |
| 1, 4096, 16 | 0.582 ms | 0.081 ms | 7.17× |
| 128, 2048, 128 (prefill) | 4.912 ms | 4.872 ms | 1.01× |

Decode across all (topk, H): median **4.30×**. Prefill: no regre

prs.title: [CI] Automate Docker Hub release image publishing
prs.author: khluu
prs.body_head:
## Summary

- Replaces manual docker tag/push/manifest commands from the release annotate script with an automated Buildkite pipeline step
- Adds a new `publish-release-images.sh` script that handles all Docker Hub publishing: CUDA 12.9, CUDA 13.0, Ubuntu 24.04 (both CUDA versions), ROCm, and CPU
- Also adds the previously missing ubuntu2404 images to the release flow

## Details

Previously, the `annotate-release.sh` script printed manual docker commands that a human had to copy-paste to publish release images to Docker Hub. This was error-prone (e.g., ubuntu2404 images were missing entirely).

This PR:
1. **New script** `.buildkite/scripts/publish-release-images.sh` — pulls images from ECR, tags with `latest` and `v$VERSION`, pushes individual arch tags, and creates multi-arch manifests for all variants
2. **New pipeline step** with a block gate in `release-pipeline.yaml` — requires manual unblock before publishing, depends on all build/manifest steps
3. **Simplified annotate script** — now only shows wheel download commands, with a note that docker images are published automatically
4. CPU images are handled gracefully — skipped with a warning if their separate block steps weren't unblocked

No existing open PR addresses this. AI assistance (Claude) was used in preparing this PR.

## Test plan

- [ ] Verify the publish script correctly tags and pushes all image variants
- [ ] Verify the block step appears in Buildkite with correct dependencies
- [ ] Verify the annotate script still renders wheel download info correctly
- [ ] Confirm CPU skip logic works when CPU block steps are not unblocked

prs.title: [Feature] TurboQuant: support hybrid models and uniform quantization
prs.author: JartX
prs.body_head:
# TurboQuant support for hybrid models

This PR fixes TurboQuant startup for hybrid models such as Qwen3.5, Qwen3-Next, and similar architectures.

Previously, TurboQuant would fail with a `NotImplementedError` as soon as it encountered Mamba layers. With this change, hybrid models are now handled correctly: TurboQuant is applied only to `full_attention` layers. 
## Additional fixes

While enabling proper hybrid support, this PR also fixes three additional issues:

- **Page-size planner mismatch:** The hybrid page-size planner was sizing attention pages using the standard formula, which does not match TurboQuant's packed K|V layout. As a result, every TurboQuant attention layer could trigger an assertion in the page merger. The planner now uses the TurboQuant-specific layout.

- **Incorrect backend selection for excluded layers:** If a layer was excluded from TurboQuant — for example because it was a skipped layer, a sliding-window layer, or a Mamba layer — the ROCm/CUDA backend selector could still incorrectly force the `TURBOQUANT` backend. These layers now correctly fall back to the default backend.

- **ROCm `flash_attn_varlen_func` incompatibility:** On ROCm, upstream `flash_attn_varlen_func` does not accept `out=`. A lightweight wrapper now detects that case and copies the result only when needed.

## Summary

Overall, this makes TurboQuant work reliably on hybrid architectures while preserving the current behavior and baselines for dense models.

prs.title: [EC Connector] Fix ECExampleConnector load device under TP>1
prs.author: miyakido
prs.body_head:
## Summary

`ECExampleConnector.start_load_caches` passes `device=current_platform.device_type` to `safetensors.torch.load_file`, which resolves to the bare string `"cuda"`. `safetensors` ignores `torch.cuda.set_device(rank)` for this case and always lands the tensor on `cuda:0`.

Under `tensor_parallel_size > 1`, this causes non-rank-0 workers to crash in `_merge_multimodal_embeddings`'s `masked_scatter_` because `inputs_embeds` lives on `cuda:<rank>` while the loaded `mm_embed` is on `cuda:0`. The exception isn't drained from the multiproc executor's shared-memory ring buffer, so every subsequent request — including text-only ones — wedges until the engine is restarted.

This was reproduced against `v0.17.0` on Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct with TP=4 on 4×L4, triggered by the first image-bearing request routed to a non-rank-0 worker.

### Fix

Pin the load to the calling rank's CUDA device instead of passing the bare device type:

```python
device = current_platform.device_type
if current_platform.is_cuda_alike():
    device = f"{device}:{torch.cuda.current_device()}"
ec_cache = safetensors.torch.load_file(filename, device=device)["ec_cache"]
```

This matches the idiom already used in `vllm/model_executor/model_loader/weight_utils.py` (lines 1047, 1098). The `is_cuda_alike()` guard preserves prior behaviour on non-CUDA platforms. The save path already does `.detach().cpu()` before writing, so it's unaffected.

### Why TP=1 didn't expose this

- `cuda:0` happens to be the right device on single-GPU prefill.
- Most public EPD deployments run prefill at TP=1 on A100-80G/H100, which can hold a 4B–8B VLM on one card. Under TP≥2 on smaller GPUs (we hit it on 4×L4 / 23 GB each), the bug is deterministic on the first image.

## Test plan

- [x] Ran `pre-commit run ruff-check --files …` — passes.
- [x] Ran `pre-commit run ruff-format --files …` — passes.
- [x] Ran `pre-commit run mypy-3.10 --files vllm/distributed/ec_transfer/ec_connector/example_connector.py --hook-stage manual`

prs.title: [Bugfix][Rocm]Aiter MoE re-uses existing tensor addresses after weight update.
prs.author: yuankaichen-amd
prs.body_head:
The AMD aiter FusedMoE requires model weights be shuffled before use. When using this feature in RL scenario, however, the MoE weights will be re-shuffled time and again whenever a weight update is carried out.

The issue is that the weight shuffle should NOT change the tensor addresses -- otherwise captured CUDA graph will not be able to use the new weights.

The fix in this PR is to preserve the weights' addresses if they already exist.

## Purpose
Aiter MoE re-uses existing tensor addresses after weight update.

## Test Plan
Test with "export VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER_MOE=1" in RL setup (e.g. veRL + vLLM, enforce_eager=False).

## Test Result
Without the fix, the generated responses are all gibberish. The fix restores the correctness.

prs.title: Fused BMM+FP8 quant Triton kernel for MLA _v_up_proj (forward_mqa path)
prs.author: dorhuri123
prs.body_head:
## Purpose

Adds a Triton kernel that fuses the batched matrix multiply in `_v_up_proj` with FP8 static quantization on the MLA decode path (`forward_mqa`), eliminating a bf16 memory round-trip.

**Depends on #36205** — will rebase onto main once that merges.

## Problem

After #36205, MLA decode quantization is two-step:
1. `_v_up_proj` does `torch.bmm` → writes bf16 to memory
2. Separate `static_scaled_fp8_quant` kernel → reads bf16, writes fp8

This is 3 memory ops (write bf16 + read bf16 + write fp8).

## Solution

A single Triton kernel (`bmm_fp8_quant`) that does the BMM and quantizes to FP8 in one pass — the bf16 intermediate never leaves GPU registers. This reduces it to 1 memory write (fp8).

### Changes
- **`vllm/v1/attention/ops/triton_bmm_fp8.py`** (new): Triton fused BMM+FP8 kernel
- **`vllm/model_executor/layers/attention/mla_attention.py`**: Modified `_v_up_proj` to accept `output_scale`/`quant_output` and call fused kernel; updated `forward_impl` to use fused path for FP8 static quant decode
- **`tests/v1/attention/ops/test_triton_bmm_fp8.py`** (new): Unit tests

### Scope
- FP8 static per-tensor quant on the standard (non-AIter) BMM path
- NVFP4 fusion for `_v_up_proj` is a follow-up (requires block-wise scales, different kernel structure)
- Prefill path (`forward_mha`) unchanged

## Test Results (H100, sm90)
```
tests/v1/attention/ops/test_triton_bmm_fp8.py::test_bmm_fp8_quant_correctness[dtype0-128-512-1-16] PASSED

prs.author: rbrugaro-amd
prs.body_head: The fuse_mla_dual_rms_norm pass (PR #39242) requires aiter.ops.fused_qk_norm_rope_cache_quant.fused_qk_rmsnorm, which was added in AITer PR #2442. The upstream Dockerfile.rocm_base pins aiter v0.1.10.post3 which does not include this kernel, causing an ImportError at runtime when the pass is auto-enabled at O1+. Add a cached availability probe (_check_aiter_fused_qk_rmsnorm) that checks whether the kernel exists in the installed aiter. The enable_mla_dual_rms_norm_fusion() default now returns False when the kernel is absent, and the runtime impl raises a clear error message if the op is somehow force-enabled without the required kernel.

prs.title: [ROCm] Add AITER RMSNorm+FP8 quantization fusion for MLA
prs.author: khairulkabir1661
prs.body_head:
## Summary

This PR adds support for AITER's fused RMSNorm + FP8 quantization kernel for DeepSeek MLA models on AMD ROCm, reducing quantization overhead.

### Key Features
- **Fused RMSNorm + FP8 Quantization**: Uses AITER's `fused_rms_fp8_group_quant` to combine RMSNorm and FP8 quantization in a single kernel
- **Pre-quantized Input Support**: Adds `input_scale`/`x_scale` parameter to linear layers to accept pre-quantized FP8 tensors
- **Reduced Quantization Steps**: Eliminates redundant quantization (3 steps → 2 steps)

## Changes

### Core Implementation
- **mla.py**: Added AITER fusion for RMSNorm + FP8 quantization when both AITER and FP8 quantization are enabled
- **linear.py**: Added `x_scale` parameter to `ReplicatedLinear`, `ColumnParallelLinear`, and `RowParallelLinear`
- **fp8.py**: Modified `Fp8LinearMethod.apply()` to accept and pass through `input_scale` parameter
- **fp8_utils.py**: Added support for pre-quantized FP8 inputs in AITER/Triton/Cutlass backends

### Testing
- Removed old test file
- Verified with GSM8K benchmark on DeepSeek-V3

## Performance Impact
- Reduces quantization overhead by fusing operations
- Avoids redundant quantization when input is already quantized
- ROCm-specific optimization for AMD GPUs

## Compatibility
- Only enabled when AITER is available AND FP8 quantization is configured
- Falls back to unfused path when AITER is not available
- No impact on other platforms or configurations

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

prs.title: [ROCm] Add HIP paged attention kernel for TurboQuant k8v4 decode
prs.author: andyluo7
prs.body_head:
## Summary

Add an optimized HIP paged attention kernel for TurboQuant k8v4 (FP8 key + 4-bit value) decode on AMD ROCm GPUs (MI300X/MI355X).

### What it does

Replaces the Triton-based TQ decode kernel with a HIP kernel on ROCm, using a hybrid dispatch:

- **Per-Q-head kernel** (grid split-K) for low batch (B≤16) or short context
- **MFMA GQA kernel** (`mfma_f32_16x16x16f16`) for high batch + long context — processes all GQA query heads simultaneously, sharing K/V loads

### Files changed (3 files, 805 insertions)

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `vllm/v1/attention/ops/tq_k8v4_rocm_decode.hip` | **New** — HIP kernel with v8 split-K + MFMA GQA + stage2 reduction |
| `vllm/v1/attention/ops/tq_k8v4_rocm_decode.py` | **New** — Python wrapper: build/cache .so, dispatch to kernel |
| `vllm/v1/attention/backends/turboquant_attn.py` | **Modified** — ROCm dispatch in `_decode_attention()` |

### What is NOT changed

- Triton TQ decode kernel (untouched)
- CUDA path (guarded by `current_platform.is_rocm()`)
- TQ store/compress ops
- No new environment variables required

prs.title: [Fix] Resolve MoRI connector hangs at high concurrency
prs.author: simondanielsson
prs.body_head:
## Purpose

Fixes #40340.

There are a few parts of the MoRI-IO connector code that can cause indefinite hangs of the connector. This PR resolves them:
1. Disable MoRI's in-band notifications (set `enable_notification=False` in `RdmaBackendConfig`) as we use ZMQ for completion notifications anyhow, and under high concurrency those notifications poison the transfer statuses because the QP send queue is exhausted (causing requests to be stuck in `WAITING_FOR_REMOTE_KVS`).
2. Handle `Failed()` transfers in _pop_done_transfers so prefill frees its blocks. These were otherwise stuck in `_recving_transfers` forever.
3. Replace asserts in `_update_from_kv_xfer_finished` with checks because async-polling connectors can deliver finished_recving/finished_sending after a request has already been removed or advanced past `WAITING_FOR_REMOTE_KVS`
4. Replace `status.Wait() ` infinite busy-spin with polling w/ deadline. 
5. Add 1ms sleep in busy-spin `while True` loops

## Test Plan

1. Build an image in this branch, including the relevant NIC drivers and userspace libraries. Or if you run on MI300X nodes with Thor2 NICs, you can pull the image I built:
```
docker pull ghcr.io/simondanielsson/vllm-rocm-moriio:dev-hang-fixes
```

2. 1p1d deployment tested with vllm bench serve @ 256 concurrency:

```shell
# Set on both nodes before running any command
export PREFILL_IP=<node1-ip>
export DECODE_IP=<node2-ip>

# Node 1 (prefill node) — command 1: start toy proxy
docker run -d \
  --name moriio-toy-proxy \
  --network host \
  ghcr.io/simondanielsson/vllm-rocm-moriio:dev-hang-fixes \
  bash -c "pip install --quiet --ignore-installed quart aiohttp msgpack && \
           python3 -u /app/vllm/examples/online_serving/disaggregated_serving/moriio_toy_proxy_server.py"

# Node 1 (prefill node) — command 2: start prefill instance
docker run -d \
  --name moriio-prefill \
  --init --network host --ipc host --privileged \
  --cap-add SYS_PTRACE --secu

prs.title: [ROCm] Add env flags to disable dynamic MXFP4 quant and enable AITER tuned GEMMs for Attention Projection Layers
prs.author: heachary
prs.body_head:
## Purpose
For models in the DeepSeek family, dynamic MXFP4 quantization is enabled by default for the attention projection layers. This behavior may be suboptimal in certain deployment scenarios.

This PR introduces two opt-in environment variables that provide finer control over this path:

1. The ability to disable dynamic MXFP4 quantization for attention projections.
2. The ability to route unquantized attention GEMMs through AITER's newer FlyDSL-based tuned GEMM implementations, instead of the default skinny-reduce or hipBLASLt paths.

In internal benchmarks, using the FlyDSL GEMM path for unquantized attention projections shows a good performance improvement on models such as Kimi-K2-Thinking.

## Summary
This PR adds two new ROCm environment variables to give operators finer control over quantization and GEMM dispatch for attention linear layers:
- **`VLLM_ROCM_DISABLE_ATTENTION_LINEAR_LAYER_DYNAMIC_MXFP4_QUANT`** (`False` by default) — When enabled, skips the dynamic MXFP4 quantization that is applied to attention projection layers (`q_proj`, `k_proj`, `v_proj`, `o_proj`) which is done upon model load for a certain family of models.
- **`VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER_TUNED_UNQUANTISED_GEMM`** (`False` by default) — When enabled, routes unquantized GEMMs in the attention path through AITER's tuned GEMM implementation (`aiter.tuned_gemm.tgemm.mm`) instead of the default skinny-reduce or hipBLASLt paths.

Both flags are opt-in and default to the current behavior, so there is no change for existing users.

## Test Result

prs.title: [ROCm][CI] Fix TestSiluMulGroupFp8QuantModel after W8A8 block linear refactor
prs.author: AndreasKaratzas
prs.body_head:
The W8A8 block linear refactor (#33892) replaced `W8A8BlockFp8LinearOp` with `TestFP8Layer` in `TestSiluMulGroupFp8QuantModel` but didn't adapt the forward call: `TestFP8Layer.forward()` takes `(y, bias=None)` while the model was still passing `(y, self.w, self.wscale)`. The redundant weight/scale tensors were also leftover from `W8A8BlockFp8LinearOp` which didn't store its own weights, unlike `TestFP8Layer` which creates them internally.

Additionally, on non-fnuz ROCm (MI355) the fusion pattern expects the triton group quant op, but the kernel's `use_triton` flag was `False` for small test shapes. Fixed by wrapping `quant_fp8` to force `use_triton=True` independently of the gemm path.

cc @kenroche

prs.title: [ROCm] Cap Triton paged attention block size to fix ROCm shared memory OOM
prs.author: AndreasKaratzas
prs.body_head:
Hybrid Mamba models (e.g. Jamba) inflate block_size to 2048 to align attention and Mamba page sizes. When the ROCm custom paged attention kernel rejects this (it only supports 16/32), the Triton fallback kernel_paged_attention_2d used 2048 as its tile size, requesting 262144 bytes of shared memory and thus exceeding the MI325X hardware limit of 65536 bytes. Cap TRITON_BLOCK_SIZE at 128. The kernel already decouples tile size from physical block size via l_block_idx/internal_offsets addressing, so this is safe.

### Test plan

- [x] `pytest tests/models/language/generation/test_hybrid.py`

cc @kenroche

prs.title: [ROCm] Cast score correction bias tensor during model construction for DeepSeek/Kimi-K2
prs.author: heachary
prs.body_head: ## Purpose The moe score correction bias tensor was being cast to the gate output dtype on every forward pass. The datatype that this tensor needs to be cast to is known at model construction time and never changes beyond that. So this repeated cast is redundant work that launches an extra GPU kernel per MoE layer per forward call. This PR moves the cast to the model construction thereby eliminating the per-forward-pass overhead. ## Summary ### Before ### After The trace shows the elementwise operation kernel responsible for this typecast operation happening before the grouped-topk operation in every forward pass. With the following changes this typecast is shifted to model construction thereby avoiding the call during every forward pass: - **`vllm/model_executor/models/deepseek_v2.py`**: Pre-cast `e_score_correction_bias` to match `gate.out_dtype` during `DeepseekV2MoE` construction. Since all downstream consumers (FusedMoE, router) share the same `nn.Parameter` object, this single mutation propagates everywhere. - **`vllm/model_executor/layers/fused_moe/rocm_aiter_fused_moe.py`**: Replace the runtime `.to()` cast with an assert that the bias dtype already matches the gating output dtype, catching any future regression where the init-time cast is missed. - **`vllm/model_executor/layers/fused_moe/router/fused_topk_bias_router.py`**: Same change — replace the runtime `.to()` cast with a matching assert. ## Test Result ### Accuracy Benchmark | Metric | Score | Threshold | Status -- | -- | -- | -- | -- GSM8K (5-shot, 250 samples) | exact_match (flexible-extract) | 0.936 | 0.90 | ✅ PASS GSM8K (5-shot, 250 samples) | exact_match (strict-match) | 0.936 | 0.90 | ✅ PASS ### Performance |

prs.title: Require C++20 for compatibility with PyTorch
prs.author: r-barnes
prs.body_head:
## Summary

PyTorch headers now enforce C++20 as a minimum (`torch/all.h` and `ATen/ATen.h` emit a `#error` if `__cplusplus < 202002L`). Building vLLM against such a PyTorch fails because vLLM's build system was still setting C++17.

- `CMakeLists.txt`: `CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD 17` → `20`
- `cmake/cpu_extension.cmake`: same
- `cmake/external_projects/deepgemm.cmake`: `-std=c++17` → `-std=c++20`

prs.title: Add VLLM_USE_SPINLOOP_EXT to use more efficient busy polling
prs.author: pschlan-amd
prs.body_head:
## Purpose

Currently, `shm_broadcast.py` is using busy polling for synchronize processes over shm. This is not ideal since it keeps the respective CPU cores 100% busy, increasing power consumption and consuming thermal budget.

Since latency has to be kept to a minimum, regular kernel-based IPC doesn't seem viable, but we can use the [`monitorx`/`mwaitx`](https://docs.amd.com/v/u/en-US/24594_3.37) instruction pair on supporting CPUs to save power while waiting. If that's not supported, we can still employ `pause` (x86) or `yield` (ARM) to give a spinloop hint to the hardware.

Since we can't invoke these instructions directly from Python, a small Python extension is required.

In my experiments, this can save up to 110 W of system power on a 8x GPU + 2x EPYC CPU machine during a tp=8 latency benchmark run.

## Risk assessment
This option has to be enabled via a new env var and is disabled by default, hence the general runtime risk should be low.
However, the build process is extended to build a new python extension, which comes with a certain risk of failing the build on more exotic targets I couldn't verify upfront. I'd hope that CI will catch those, though.

## Test Plan
- Run vLLM e.g. with tp=8 and mp distributed backend and measure the system's power consumption when idle
- Run vLLM latency benchmark
- Set env var VLLM_USE_SPINLOOP_EXT=1
- Run measurements again

prs.title: [ROCm] Add MLA dual RMS norm fusion (Q, KV) pass for DeepSeek/Kimi-K2
prs.author: rbrugaro-amd
prs.body_head:
## Summary

Add a `torch.inductor` post-grad pattern-matcher pass that fuses the paired `q_a_layernorm` and `kv_a_layernorm` RMS norm operations in DeepSeek-V3 / Kimi-K2 MLA attention into a single `fused_qk_rmsnorm` HIP kernel call via AITer.

In the unfused graph, each MLA layer runs two separate `rms_norm` calls on the q and kv compressed latents:

```
qkv_lora = gemm(hidden)
q_c, kv_lora = split(qkv_lora, [1536, 576])
kv_c, k_pe   = split(kv_lora,  [512,  64])
q_c  = rms_norm(q_c,  q_weight,  eps)   # norm #1
kv_c = rms_norm(kv_c, kv_weight, eps)   # norm #2
```

The pass uses `torch._inductor.pattern_matcher.register_replacement` to match the connected subgraph — both splits and both norms — and rewrites it to:

```
q_c, kv_lora = split(qkv_lora, [1536, 576])
kv_c, k_pe   = split(kv_lora,  [512,  64])
q_normed, kv_normed = fused_mla_dual_rms_norm(
    q_c, q_weight, kv_c, kv_weight, eps1, eps2)
```

This reduces kernel launch overhead from 2 launches to 1 per MLA layer (61 layers in Kimi-K2).

### Changes

- **`vllm/_aiter_ops.py`** — register `fused_mla_dual_rms_norm` custom op wrapping AITer's `fused_qk_rmsnorm` HIP kernel
- **`vllm/compilation/passes/fusion/rocm_aiter_fusion.py`** — `MLADualRMSNormPattern` + `MLADualRMSNormFusionPass` using `register_replacement` pattern matching
- **`vllm/config/compilation.py`** / **`vllm/config/vllm.py`** — `fuse_mla_dual_rms_norm` PassConfig flag (ROCm-only, auto-enabled when AITer is available at O1+)
- **`vllm/compilation/passes/pass_manager.py`** — register the pass
- **`tests/compile/passes/test_fuse_mla_dual_rms_norm.py`** — unit test verifying pattern match, op replacement, and numerical correctness

### Enable

Enabled by default when AITer is available (optimization level ≥ O1). Can also be toggled explicitly:

```bash
--compilation-config='{"pass_config": {"fuse_mla_dual_rms_norm": true}}'
```

### AITer dependency

Requires AITer PR [#2442](https://github.com/ROCm/aite

prs.title: [ROCm][Feature] Enable AITER MLA attention backend to work with Eagle3 speculative decoding on ROCm
prs.author: larryli2-amd
prs.body_head:
## Summary                                                                                      
                                                                                                  
  Enable AITER MLA attention backend to work with Eagle3 speculative decoding on ROCm.            
                                                                                                  
  AITER MLA is the fastest MLA attention backend on AMD MI300X/MI355X GPUs (using AMD's ASM       
  kernels via the aiter library). However, it currently cannot be used with speculative decoding
  methods like Eagle3 due to a `kernel_block_size` conflict:                                      
                                                                                                
  - **AITER MLA** declares `get_supported_kernel_block_sizes() = [1]`                             
  - **Eagle3 draft model** (flash_attn) requires `block_size = MultipleOf(16)`                    
  - Both share the same KV cache group, so `select_common_block_size()` fails — no block size      satisfies both constraints                                                                      
                                                 
  This means users must choose between AITER MLA (fast decode, no spec-dec) or a slower backend   
  (spec-dec works but lower baseline performance). This PR removes that limitation.               
                                                                                                  
  ## Key Insight                                                                                  
                                                 
  The aiter MLA decode kernel always operates with `page_size=1` internally — the vLLM wrapper    
  flattens `kv_buffer` via `.view(-1, 1, 1, head_size)` before calling the ASM kernel. The kernel
  never sees multi-token pages. So AITER MLA can work with any `kernel_block_size` — we just need 
  to exp

prs.title: [ROCm] Enable building MoRI with AINIC and Broadcom bnxt (Thor2) NIC stack
prs.author: haic0
prs.body_head:
## Summary
Updates the ROCm image build (docker/Dockerfile.rocm and docker/Dockerfile.rocm_base) so MORI can be built with an optional AMD AINIC (Pensando / ionic) or **BNXT (broadcom)" NIC stack, following the same approach as SGLang’s [docker/rocm.Dockerfile](https://github.com/sgl-project/sglang/blob/main/docker/rocm.Dockerfile#L366) MORI section.


Co-authored with: @ichbinblau @simondanielsson

This PR adds support for installing Thor2 drivers and userspace libraries directly into Dockerfile.rocm. This way we can build images vllm/vllm-openai-rocm:nightly-<commit-id> that can run MoRI-IO on AINIC (Pensando / ionic) and Thor2 devices.
Also bumps the default MORI git pin to v1.1.0.
Upgrade the RDMA version to the latest 60.0. 

## Usage :
(build vLLM ROCm image with With AINIC / ionic: backend):

DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build \
          --build-arg max_jobs=16 \
          --build-arg NIC_BACKEND=ainic \
          --build-arg BASE_IMAGE=rocm/vllm-dev:base \
          --tag  rocm/vllm-dev:latest \
          -f docker/Dockerfile.rocm .

(build vLLM ROCm image with Broadcom Thor2 bnxt NIC backend):

 docker build -f docker/Dockerfile.rocm \
 --build-arg NIC_BACKEND=bnxt \
  -t vllm/vllm-openai-rocm:nightly-<commit-id>-bxnt .


## Motivation
Upgrade MoRI to v1.1.0
Support NIC_BACKEND=bnxt  or ainic when building the MORI wheel so users targeting Thor NICs get libionic-dev / ionic-common.
Install common MoriIO proxy Python packages in the dev base image for disaggregated / KV workflows that use the proxy.

## How to build

With AINIC / ionic:

DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build \
          --build-arg max_jobs=16 \
          --build-arg NIC_BACKEND=ainic \
          --build-arg BASE_IMAGE=rocm/vllm-dev:base \
          --tag  rocm/vllm-dev:latest \
          -f docker/Dockerfile.rocm .

With broadcom NIC: 
  docker build -f docker/Dockerfile.rocm \
  --build-arg NIC_BACKEND=bnxt \
 -t vllm/vllm-openai-rocm:nightly-<commit-id>

prs.title: attention: add USE_TD constexpr for tensor descriptor Q/K/V load/store
prs.author: afierka-intel
prs.body_head:
## Summary

Add a `USE_TD: tl.constexpr` flag to both `kernel_unified_attention_2d` and `kernel_unified_attention_3d` in the Triton unified attention backend. When enabled, Q/K/V loads and output stores use `tl.make_tensor_descriptor` (HW 2D block reads on Intel Xe2/Xe3). The dead branch is eliminated at Triton compile time, so NVIDIA/AMD see zero overhead.

## Changes

### `vllm/v1/attention/ops/triton_unified_attention.py`

- `_load_q_td()` — helper: loads Q tensor via 2D/3D tensor descriptor, handles `HEAD_SIZE == HEAD_SIZE_PADDED` branching
- `_load_kv_tile_td()` — helper: loads K or V cache tile via descriptor, transposes K when `IS_K=True`, uses `shape=HEAD_SIZE` with `block_shape=HEAD_SIZE_PADDED` for automatic zero-padding
- `_load_kv_tile_ptr()` — helper: loads K or V cache tile via pointer arithmetic (extracted from inline code for clarity)
- `_store_output_2d_td()` — helper: stores output via tensor descriptor for 2D kernel
- `_store_segm_output_3d_td()` — helper: stores segment output via tensor descriptor for 3D kernel
- Both 2D and 3D kernels: `USE_TD: tl.constexpr = False` parameter, `tl.static_assert(BLOCK_SIZE % TILE_SIZE == 0)` guard
- `unified_attention()` wrapper: accepts `use_td: bool = False`, passes to kernels

### `vllm/v1/attention/backends/triton_attn.py`

- Auto-detect: `self.use_td = current_platform.is_xpu()`
- Override via `VLLM_TRITON_USE_TD` env var (registered in `vllm/envs.py`)

### `vllm/envs.py`

- Register `VLLM_TRITON_USE_TD`: `None` = auto-detect, `True`/`False` = explicit override

prs.title: [ROCm] route known-bad gfx9 ROCM_ATTN mfma4 shapes to Triton
prs.author: Bortlesboat
prs.body_head:
## Summary
ROCM_ATTN on gfx9 currently routes `gqa_ratio=2` and `gqa_ratio=4` decoder shapes through the native `mfma4` paged-attention path in V1. Two open ROCm issues point at that same path:

- `#35569`: deterministic Qwen3-VL reranker score drift on gfx950 (`head_size=128`, `gqa_ratio=2`)
- `#36180`: `meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct` illegal memory access during graph capture on gfx942 (`head_size=64`, `gqa_ratio=4`)

This keeps `ROCM_ATTN` selected, but skips those known-bad gfx9 `mfma4` specializations so `chunked_prefill_paged_decode` falls back to the existing Triton implementation for those shapes.

## Why this is not a duplicate
I checked both issue threads plus open PR searches for `35569 in:body`, `36180 in:body`, `ROCM_ATTN Qwen3-VL-Reranker`, and `ROCM_ATTN Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct`. I did not find an open PR already routing these gfx9 `mfma4` shapes away from the native kernel.

prs.title: [Attention Backend] TurboQuant: 2-bit KV cache compression with 4x capacity
prs.author: vibhavagarwal5
prs.body_head:
### Summary

TurboQuant adds **online KV cache compression** to vLLM's v1 attention backend using PolarQuant (WHT rotation + Lloyd-Max scalar quantization) for keys and uniform quantization for values. All quantization happens at store time via fused Triton kernels — no offline calibration, model changes, or weight modifications required. Just set `--kv-cache-dtype turboquant_k8v4`.

### Compression Presets (Qwen3-4B, head_dim=128)

| Preset | Key | Value | Slot (bytes) | Compression | GSM8K | NIAH |
|--------|-----|-------|-------------|-------------|-------|------|
| `turboquant_k8v4` | FP8 (E4M3) | 4-bit uniform | 196 | **2.6x** | 0.860 | 100% |
| `turboquant_4bit_nc` | 4-bit MSE + NC | 4-bit uniform + NC | 136 | **3.8x** | 0.840 | 100% |
| `turboquant_k3v4_nc` | 3-bit MSE + NC | 4-bit uniform + NC | 120 | **4.3x** | 0.780 | 100% |
| `turboquant_3bit_nc` | 3-bit MSE + NC | 3-bit uniform + NC | 104 | **4.9x** | 0.720 | 100% |

Baseline: GSM8K 0.900, NIAH 100%. Measured on Qwen/Qwen3-4B with 5-shot GSM8K (200q) and NIAH (512-32K, 77 probes).

prs.title: [ROCm][Engine] Fix GPU memory leaks in engine shutdown and test workaround for async KV prefix cache reset
prs.author: AndreasKaratzas
prs.body_head:
When running in-process (`VLLM_ENABLE_V1_MULTIPROCESSING=0`), shutting down the engine leaked GPU memory because:

- **`supports_kw` LRU cache pinned model weights**: The `@lru_cache` on `supports_kw` was keyed on bound methods (e.g. `model.forward`), which held strong references to the model instance and all its GPU tensors for the lifetime of the cache. Fixed by unwrapping bound methods to `__func__` before caching.
- **`gc.freeze()` was never undone**: `EngineCore.__init__` calls `gc.freeze()` to reduce GC overhead during serving, but `shutdown()` never called `gc.unfreeze()`, making all engine objects invisible to the garbage collector.
- **No explicit GPU resource teardown**: `GPUWorker.shutdown()` never freed model weights, KV caches, the rotary embedding cache (`_ROPE_DICT`), compilation contexts, or the workspace singleton.

### Test workaround for prefix cache reset

`reset_prefix_cache` could return `False` indefinitely when async KV store transfers (CPU offloading) were in flight, because the engine stops stepping after all requests finish and never polls the worker for completed transfers. As a temporary workaround (per reviewer suggestion), `_wait_for_prefix_cache_reset` in the test now sends dummy single-token prefills between retries to force the engine to step, which naturally drains completed transfers and frees GPU blocks.

### UCX fix

Bump UCX branch in ROCm Dockerfile to `33b3b2a2` which fixes a memory leak in UCX when used with NIXL.

## Test plan

- [x] `pytest tests/v1/kv_offload/ tests/v1/kv_connector/unit/ tests/v1/core/test_scheduler.py tests/v1/core/test_prefix_caching.py tests/v1/core/test_reset_prefix_cache_e2e.py tests/v1/simple_kv_offload/test_integration.py -v`

### [LEGACY] Initial Test plan and Motivation

- [x] `pytest tests/v1/kv_offload/test_cpu_offloading.py::test_cpu_offloading[TRITON_ATTN-48]`

Motivation: https://buildkite.com/vllm/amd-ci/builds/7062/steps/canvas?sid=019d382d-caab-452a-bf83-1c611ef498b6

prs.title: [ROCm] Add gfx1102/gfx1103 support
prs.author: mgehre-amd
prs.body_head:
gfx1103 (RDNA 3, e.g. Radeon 780M iGPU) was missing from the HIP_SUPPORTED_ARCHS list in CMakeLists.txt and from the compile-time architecture macros in skinny_gemms.cu and attention.cu.

The skinny GEMM and attention kernels defined __HIP__GFX1X__ and __HIP__GFX11__ by listing individual arch targets (__gfx1100__, __gfx1101__, __gfx1150__, __gfx1151__) but missed __gfx1103__. This caused wvSplitK kernel bodies to compile as UNREACHABLE_CODE (assert false) on gfx1103 while the wave32 reduction code was still active, resulting in a crash at runtime.

Replace __HIP__GFX11__ / __HIP__GFX12__ with the compiler-provided __GFX11__ / __GFX12__ umbrella macros which are defined for every target in each family. This avoids needing to enumerate individual targets and automatically covers future gfx11xx/gfx12xx additions.

prs.title: [ROCm][CI] Add missing quantization methods and fix online quant test failures
prs.author: AndreasKaratzas
prs.body_head:
Several quantization methods were failing on ROCm because they weren't registered in the platform's `supported_quantization` list, even though the underlying kernels already support them (either natively or through emulation fallbacks).

This adds `fp8_per_tensor`, `fp8_per_block`, `online`, `gpt_oss_mxfp4`, `mxfp8`, `modelopt`, `modelopt_mxfp8`, and `modelopt_mixed` to ROCm's supported quantization allowlist. These all have working codepaths on ROCm. FP8 variants use `ROCmFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel`, mxfp8 and modelopt_mxfp8 use `EmulationMxfp8LinearKernel` (introduced in #39205), and the online quant schemes resolve to fp8 methods that already work.

Also fixes two issues in the test infrastructure that were blocking the online quantization peak memory test on ROCm:

1. The peak GPU memory log in `base_loader.py` was gated on `is_cuda()` only, so it never fired on ROCm. Changed to `is_cuda_alike()` which covers both CUDA and ROCm since `torch.accelerator.max_memory_allocated()` works on both.

2. The memory thresholds in `test_online_quant_peak_mem` were hardcoded for CUDA's `fp8_e4m3fn`. ROCm on gfx950 uses `fp8_e4m3fnuz` which results in slightly higher memory usage (~7.45 GiB vs ~6.65 GiB), so the thresholds are now platform-aware.

cc @kenroche

prs.title: [ROCm] Widen OAI Triton MoE capability range to include gfx12 (RDNA4)
prs.author: laudney
prs.body_head:
## Summary

- Widen `_supports_current_device()` cap range from `< (11, 0)` to `< (13, 0)` in `BaseOAITritonExperts` and `OAITritonMxfp4ExpertsMonolithic`
- Same change in `oracle/mxfp4.py` for the LoRA path

gfx12 (RDNA4) maps to capability `(12, 0)` which was excluded by the old upper bound. Triton 3.5+ supports `tl.dot_scaled` on gfx12 via `DecomposeScaledBlocked`, so the standard MXFP4 MoE path works without any custom kernel.

Replaces #34632 which tried to add a custom dequant kernel — turns out that's unnecessary (thanks @ptrojahn for pushing back on that).

Tested with `openai/gpt-oss-20b` (MXFP4 MoE) on AMD Radeon AI PRO R9700 (gfx1201).

AI assistance was used. Test commands run: `curl localhost:8080/v1/chat/completions` against a running vLLM instance with the model loaded.

## Test plan

- [x] Load MXFP4 MoE model (gpt-oss-20b) on gfx1201 — model loads, correct output
- [ ] CI

prs.title: Rocm/fused short seq attention
prs.author: MohamedSayedFathy
prs.body_head:
<!-- markdownlint-disable -->

## Purpose

Optimize PagedAttention v2 decode performance on ROCm for short sequences (≤256 tokens) by eliminating the reduce kernel when only a single partition is needed.

When `max_num_partitions == 1`, the QKV kernel writes directly to `final_out` instead of `tmp_out`, skipping:
- The `tmp_out` intermediate buffer write (~8 KB per seq/layer)
- The `tmp_out` read-back in the reduce kernel (~8 KB per seq/layer)
- The `exp_sums`/`max_logits` metadata writes (~256 bytes per seq/layer)
- The reduce kernel launch entirely (~10μs overhead)

This saves ~16.5 KB of HBM traffic per sequence per layer and eliminates one kernel launch.

### Key design choices:
- Uses `if constexpr (SINGLE_PARTITION)` for zero-cost abstraction — dead code eliminated at compile time
- Enabled by default, togglable via `VLLM_ROCM_FUSED_SHORT_SEQ=0` for A/B benchmarking
- Applied to both mfma4 (GQA ≤ 4) and mfma16 (GQA 5-16) kernels on MI300X
- No changes to the multi-partition path — long sequences are unaffected

## Test Plan

Verified on AMD MI300X by running `benchmark_paged_attention.py` with both `VLLM_ROCM_FUSED_SHORT_SEQ=1` (optimized) and `VLLM_ROCM_FUSED_SHORT_SEQ=0` (baseline). Both paths produce correct results with matching outputs.

```bash
# Optimized path (default)
python benchmarks/kernels/benchmark_paged_attention.py \
  --version v2 --custom-paged-attn --seq-len 128 --batch-size 8

# Baseline path (disabled)
VLLM_ROCM_FUSED_SHORT_SEQ=0 python benchmarks/kernels/benchmark_paged_attention.py \
  --version v2 --custom-paged-attn --seq-len 128 --batch-size 8

prs.title: [XPU] update dp rank w/o env-var isolation
prs.author: zhenwei-intel
prs.body_head:
## Purpose
The old vLLM worker handles DP on XPU by calling set_device_control_env_var to set ZE_AFFINITY_MASK, so each DP worker only sees a device subset. In practice, this approach can introduce accuracy issues when DP+EP.

Example (ZE_AFFINITY_MASK='2,3'):
- ZE_AFFINITY_MASK='2' for DP0, using torch.device("xpu:0")
- ZE_AFFINITY_MASK='3' for DP1, also using torch.device("xpu:0")



This PR switches XPU worker device assignment to follow the same approach as the [GPU worker](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/blob/c0ecaed95030815c49699995bf6461209ccd6a0f/vllm/v1/worker/gpu_worker.py#L241) 
where the device is assigned using:
`DP_LOCAL_RANK * TP_PP_WORLD_SIZE + TP_LOCAL_RANK`

Example (ZE_AFFINITY_MASK='2,3'):
- DP0, using torch.device("xpu:0")
- DP1, using torch.device("xpu:1")

prs.title: [NVFP4][Hopper/AMD Instinct] Add Triton kernels for NVFP4 dequantization and QDQ emulation
prs.author: fxmarty-amd
prs.body_head:
## Purpose

Add Triton kernels for NVFP4 dequantization and quantize-dequantize emulation to accelerate NVFP4 quantization emulation on devices that do not support this dtype natively.

The Triton kernels automatically replace the torch eager reference implementation when running on CUDA devices, with significant perf improvements, and avoids launching many independent kernel when using `--enforce-eager`, with back and forth HBM read/write.

These fused kernels  also help to stop hitting https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/180341 and https://github.com/ROCm/rocm-systems/issues/5071 where many stacked kernels calls from `dequantize_to_dtype` and `ref_nvfp4_quant_dequant` result in HSA crashes in recent ROCm versions / pytorch nightly (`vllm bench serve` with NVFP4 emulation on MI355X is not runnable without `HSA_TOOLS_DISABLE_REGISTER=1` due to these crashes - adding this env var is very slow).


This PR is submitted independently from https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/pull/35737, but closely related to it.

## Test Plan

```python
pytest tests/models/quantization/test_nvfp4.py::test_triton_dequantize_nvfp4 -vvvvv -s 
pytest tests/models/quantization/test_nvfp4.py::test_triton_nvfp4_quant_dequant -vvvvv -s
```

## Test Result

The tests verify:
1. Correctness: Triton kernels produce bitwise-identical results to CPU reference (atol=0, rtol=0)
2. Coverage: Tests multiple tensor shapes (2D/3D), sizes, and global scale values
3. Real weights: Dequantization test uses actual NVFP4 weights from `nvidia/Qwen3-30B-A3B-NVFP4` checkpoint
4. Performance: Benchmarks show speedup vs. CPU reference implementation

## `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=7 pytest tests/models/quantization/test_nvfp4.py -s -vvvvv -k "test_triton_dequantize_nvfp4"`

```
  dequantize 2D base [512, 1024]:
    triton:    median=0.007ms, min=0.007ms, max=0.008ms
    reference: median=0.088ms, min=0.073ms, max=0.256ms
    speedup:   12.63x
  dequantize 2D 2x rows [1024, 1024]:

prs.title: [ROCm][CI] Add K8s-hardened minimal Python CI runner with JUnit exit-code fix and resource watchdog
prs.author: AndreasKaratzas
prs.body_head:
Replaces the 500-line bash `run-amd-test.sh` with Python script (`run-amd-test.py`) that is easier to read, debug, and extend. The bash script becomes a thin shim that delegates to the Python implementation.

The new script handles:
- `amd-smi` GPU integrity verification
- Docker pull with retry (3 attempts, exponential backoff)
- Container execution with configurable timeout; kills and returns exit code 124 on timeout
- Health watchdog with background thread samples memory, host memory, disk usage, GPU VRAM, and container state every 60s. It also writes pre/post snapshots and all samples to `health_watchdog.log`
- JUnit exit-code override that promotes exit 0 to 1 when `results.xml` reports failures (catches `os._exit(0)` masking real failures)

cc @kenroche @okakarpa @dhonnappa-amd @tjtanaa

prs.title: [ROCm][CI] Optimize ROCm Docker build: registry cache, DeepEP, and ci-bake script
prs.author: AndreasKaratzas
prs.body_head:
## Summary

Implements the three-tier Docker build for ROCm CI. Every PR currently rebuilds RIXL, DeepEP, rocshmem, torchcodec, and RDMA libraries from scratch, costing a total of 26 minutes on average per build. This PR introduces a pre-built Tier-1 `ci_base` image that absorbs those stable layers. Per-PR builds then only rebuild the thin vLLM wheel + workspace layer.

**Image registry layout after this PR:**

| Tag | Built by | Frequency |
|-----|----------|-----------|
| `rocm/vllm-dev:base` | `Dockerfile.rocm_base` | Monthly |
| `rocm/vllm-dev:ci_base` | `ci_base` stage (this PR) | Weekly |
| `rocm/vllm-ci:$COMMIT` | `test` stage (this PR) | Per PR |

---

This PR is connected to: https://github.com/vllm-project/ci-infra/pull/307
These two PRs should likely be merged simultaneously.

prs.title: [ROCm] Fix AssertionError in ActivationQuantFusionPass when torch.compile is used on ROCm
prs.author: Bortlesboat
prs.body_head:
PR #38817 changed the `SiluMulBlockQuantPattern` registration guard from `is_cuda()` to `is_cuda_alike()`, inadvertently enabling it on ROCm. However, this pattern requires `per_token_group_fp8_quant` in `QUANT_OPS` (from `rms_quant_fusion.py`), and that op is CUDA-only (`#ifndef USE_ROCM` in `csrc/libtorch_stable/torch_bindings.cpp`).

On ROCm, `QUANT_OPS` does not contain `kFp8Dynamic128Sym` or `kFp8Dynamic64Sym`, so `ActivationQuantFusionPass.__init__` crashes when torch.compile is used:
```
AssertionError: unsupported quantization scheme kFp8Dynamic128Sym
```

The pattern can never match on ROCm anyway since the unfused `per_token_group_fp8_quant` op does not exist there. ROCm uses `rocm_aiter_fusion.py` for its own block-quant fusions.

Fix: revert the `SiluMulBlockQuantPattern` loop guard to `is_cuda()`. The `FUSED_OPS` entry for `silu_and_mul_per_block_quant` remains `is_cuda_alike()` for potential future use.

**Duplicate check:** No existing open PR addresses this. This is a regression introduced in #38817 (merged Apr 8, 2026).

**Tests:** `ActivationQuantFusionPass` is only instantiated during `torch.compile`; verified the guard logic by tracing `QUANT_OPS` population in `rms_quant_fusion.py` (lines 82-84, CUDA-only) against the `is_cuda_alike()` check.

**AI assistance:** Authored with Claude Code assistance.

cc @jeffdaily @sunway513 @jithunnair-amd @pruthvistony @ROCmSupport @gshtras @hongxiayang @naromero77amd

prs.title: [Core] Disable HMA for eagle/MTP with sliding window models
prs.author: Bortlesboat
prs.body_head:
Models like Step-3.5-Flash interleave sliding window and full attention layers. When HMA is enabled it splits them into separate KV cache groups, causing `validate_same_kv_cache_group()` in the eagle/MTP drafter to fail:

```
AssertionError: All drafting layers should belong to the same kv cache group
```

This adds a guard to auto-disable HMA when eagle/MTP speculative decoding is combined with a sliding window model, following the same pattern as the existing guard for chunked local attention + eagle at line 1245.

Fixes #38498

**Duplicate check:** No existing open PR addresses this. Previous PRs and issues discuss the symptom but no fix was merged.

**Tests:** Verified the guard logic matches the existing `chunked_prefill_enabled` pattern; unit tests for `VllmConfig` pass with `VLLM_USE_PRECOMPILED=1 uv pip install -e .`.

prs.title: [ROCm] Use unified decode fallback for sliding-window AITER FA
prs.author: Bortlesboat
prs.body_head:
## Summary

The decode path in `rocm_aiter_fa.py` already documents that sliding-window attention should bypass `paged_attention_v1` and use the unified attention fallback, but the condition only checked small head sizes. This patch moves that rule into a small helper, applies it in the decode branch, and adds a narrow regression test that locks the sliding-window case in.

## Why this isn't duplicate work

- Checked nearby ROCm issue and PR activity for the same area, including `gh pr list --repo vllm-project/vllm --state open --search "sliding window unified_attention ROCM_AITER_FA"`
- Checked adjacent `rocm_aiter_fa` lanes such as `#33761`, `#39192`, and `#39527`
- The open work I found in this file is about KV-cache layout or broader kernel changes; I did not find an open PR that covers this decode fallback condition.

## Test Plan

- `wsl -e bash --noprofile --norc -lc 'cd /mnt/c/Users/andre/.config/superpowers/worktrees/vllm/vllm-rocm-aiter-fa-sliding-window-fallback && .venv/bin/python -m pytest --noconftest tests/v1/attention/test_rocm_aiter_fa_utils.py -q'`
- `wsl -e bash --noprofile --norc -lc 'cd /mnt/c/Users/andre/.config/superpowers/worktrees/vllm/vllm-rocm-aiter-fa-sliding-window-fallback && .venv/bin/python -m py_compile vllm/v1/attention/backends/rocm_aiter_fa.py vllm/v1/attention/backends/rocm_aiter_fa_utils.py tests/v1/attention/test_rocm_aiter_fa_utils.py'`
- `git diff --check`

## Test Result

## AI assistance

prs.title: [ROCm] Fix cu_seqlens_q off-by-one in AITER FA speculative decode path
prs.author: Bortlesboat
prs.body_head:
In the speculative decode path of `AiterFlashAttentionImpl` (when `decode_max_query_len > 1`), `cu_seqlens_q` is sliced as `query_start_loc[:num_decodes]`, giving `num_decodes` elements. But `cu_seqlens_q` for `unified_attention` is a cumulative length array that needs `num_seqs + 1` entries (including the leading 0).

The correct pattern is already used in the fallback path 33 lines later (line 1228-1229), which correctly does `[:num_decodes + 1]`.

Also fixed `descale_shape` which computed `shape[0] - 1` on the wrong-length slice, producing `num_decodes - 1` instead of `num_decodes`. Simplified to use `num_decodes` directly, matching line 1232.

No duplicate PRs found.

Testing:
- `uvx pre-commit run ruff-format --files vllm/v1/attention/backends/rocm_aiter_fa.py` (passes locally)
- Previous CI failure only requested the matching `ruff format` whitespace change on this line; this follow-up commit applies that exact diff.

AI assistance was used for code search and CI log review; all changes reviewed by the human submitter.

Signed-off-by: Bortlesboat <bortstheboat@gmail.com>

**Reference:** The correct `cu_seqlens_q` slicing is confirmed by the upstream AITER `unified_attention` implementation:
https://github.com/ROCm/aiter/blob/bc5ea32c19c604cda2f4781a97cb04d5fc494543/aiter/ops/triton/_triton_kernels/attention/unified_attention.py#L426

When using LoRA adapters with Nemotron Nano BF16:
https://huggingface.co/nvidia/NVIDIA-Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B-BF16

prs.title: [Feature] limit thinking tokens (hard limit)
prs.author: llsj14
prs.body_head:
## Essential Elements of an Effective PR Description Checklist
- [x] The purpose of the PR, such as "Fix some issue (link existing issues this PR will resolve)".
- [x] The test plan, such as providing test command.
- [x] The test results, such as pasting the results comparison before and after, or e2e results
- [x] (Optional) The necessary documentation update, such as updating `supported_models.md` and `examples` for a new model.

## Related Issue:
- #15418
- #17887
- #25714
- #26091
- Recently Nemotron 3 shows reasoning budget control with multiple API calls (https://huggingface.co/nvidia/NVIDIA-Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B-BF16#using-budget-control)

## Purpose
- Support limiting thinking tokens and forcing thinking end tokens via logit processors.
- This feature is designed to prevent uncontrolled long reasoning loops and to enforce an explicit thinking limit (budget), which is a form of hard limiting. This is different from reasoning_level and the soft limiting technique used in gpt-oss.
- This PR aims to address service pain points:
  - The soft limiting technique (e.g.,` reasoning_level` in `gpt-oss`) is only available in certain models. Without such models, controlling thinking tokens in the current vLLM implementation requires making two separate API calls. ([Example](https://qwen.readthedocs.io/en/latest/getting_started/quickstart.html#thinking-budget) with the Qwen model.) This is a major pain point because, when making API calls twice, there is no guarantee they will be routed to the same server node unless the system has prefix aware routing.
  - Even with soft limiting techniques based on prompting, models often generate repetitive reasoning content or produce tokens related to the prompt instructions themselves, which negatively affects output quality. This issue has been reported by the service team, and it highlights the clear need for a hard limit in addition to soft limiting.
- discussions: https://vllm-dev.slack.com/archives/C07QQ8DAXMK/p1755614191019

prs.title: [ZenCPU] AMD Zen CPU Backend with supported dtypes via zentorch weekly
prs.author: Chinmay-Kulkarni-AMD
prs.body_head:
## Purpose
Update the AMD Zen CPU platform (`ZenCpuPlatform`) to correctly declare supported dtypes and switch the zentorch dependency to the weekly release channel.
This PR fixes the two minor issues introduced in https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/issues/35089
1. **Declare `supported_dtypes` for Zen CPU**: AMD Zen CPUs do not support float16 compute natively. The base `CpuPlatform` includes `torch.float16` in its `supported_dtypes`, which causes vLLM to accept float16 models on Zen CPUs. This leads to dtype mismatches and the execution errors out. This PR overrides `supported_dtypes` in `ZenCpuPlatform` to return only `[torch.bfloat16, torch.float32]`, matching the actual hardware capabilities.
2. **Switch `vllm[zen]` to `zentorch-weekly`**: The `zentorch` PyPI release cadence is weekly. Using `zentorch-weekly` ensures `pip install vllm[zen]` always pulls the known-good version of weekly build instead of release builds.
## Test Plan
- Verify that models with native float16 dtype (e.g., `bloom-560m`, `opt-125m`) are automatically resolved to bfloat16 on Zen CPU with a warning, instead of silently running in an unsupported dtype.
- Run the existing CPU generation tests on an AMD Zen machine:
  ```bash
  pytest -v -s tests/models/language/generation -m cpu_model
- Verify pip install vllm[zen] resolves to the latest zentorch-weekly package.
- Verify that the test cases added specifically for Zen CPU and zentorch can run and pass.
  ```bash
  pytest tests/test_zen_cpu_platform_detection.py -v
  pytest tests/model_executor/test_cpu_unquantized_gemm_dispatch.py -v
 
## Test Result
- Models with torch_dtype=float16 in their HF config are correctly downcast to bfloat16 with a log warning: Your device 'cpu' doesn't support torch.float16. Falling back to torch.bfloat16 for compatibility.
- Models with torch_dtype=bfloat16 or torch_dtype=float32 are unaffected.
- pip install "vllm[zen]" successfully installs zentorch-weekly.
- Test cases added specific

prs.title: [ROCm][CI] Build fastsafetensors from source so it links against libamdhip64
prs.author: AndreasKaratzas
prs.body_head:
The `fastsafetensors` wheel on PyPI is CUDA-only. Its compiled extension `dlopen`s `libcudart.so` unconditionally, so on ROCm hosts it throws:

```
Exception: [FAIL] GPU runtime library (libcudart.so or libamdhip64.so) does not exist
```

even though `libamdhip64.so` is present. This breaks both the ROCm CI test image and the ROCm release image today. Installing it directly from the pinned git tag forces build from source. Resolves failure in `mi250_1: Model Executor` test group:

```bash
pytest tests/model_executor/model_loader/fastsafetensors_loader/
```

cc @kenroche

prs.title: [CPU] Fix AttributeError when loading GeluAndMul and similar activations
prs.author: ssam18
prs.body_head: When the vLLM CPU C++ extension fails to load (e.g. due to a PyTorch ABI mismatch), `torch.ops._C` activation ops like `gelu_tanh_and_mul` are never registered, causing `GeluAndMul`, `NewGELU`, `FastGELU`, and `QuickGELU` to crash at model init with an `AttributeError`. The fix removes `is_cpu()` from the op-access guard in each class since the CPU forward path already falls back to the native PyTorch implementation through `forward_cpu` --> `forward_native`, so `self.op` was dead code on CPU anyway. Added a regression test that verifies the classes initialize and produce correct output on CPU even when the C extension is absent. Fixes #39334

prs.title: [Bugfix][Rocm] fix qr error when different inp shape
prs.author: haoyangli0109
prs.body_head:
When the tensor parallelism (TP) degree is set to 4 or 8, frequent changes in the input shape can cause QuickReduce to hang (this issue has been observed with the gpt_oss model).
We have identified that the root cause is overlapping flag memory addresses between consecutive AllReduce operations.

For most models, the hidden size remains relatively stable, so this issue does not occur.

Our current solution is to allocate separate memory regions for the flags and data of the two AllReduce phases in each operation.
(Note: The data region must also be separated, as overlapping would lead to correctness issues.)

**To reproduce error**

1.git clone https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm.git
2.python3 setup.py develop
3.python3 this_script.py
```

prs.body_head:
For fp8 quantization, the [scale.dim()=2 ](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/blob/main/vllm/_custom_ops.py#L1729)returned by pertensor's input quantization causes errors during inference.
The occurrence of this issue is also related to the judgment conditions of pertoken. https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/blob/main/vllm/model_executor/layers/quantization/utils/w8a8_utils.py#L477 



Script to Reproduce the Error
```
# SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0

from vllm import LLM, SamplingParams

import argparse
prompts = [
    "Hello, my name is",
    "The president of the United States is",
    "The capital of France is",
    "The future of AI is",
]
sampling_params = SamplingParams(temperature=0.8, top_p=0.95)
if __name__ == "__main__":
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--dtype", type=str, default="auto", choices=["auto", "float16", "bfloat16", "float32"], help="Data type for model weights and computation.")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    outputs = llm.generate(prompts, sampling_params)

```

prs.title: [Model][Hardware][AMD][Kernel]: Enable e2e QK Norm + RoPE + KV Cache runtime fusion for Qwen3-30B-A3B on ROCM_ATTN, ROCM_AITER_FA, and ROCM_AITER_UNIFIED_ATTN
prs.author: jhu960213
prs.body_head:
## Purpose

- Enable e2e runtime QK Norm + RoPE + KV Cache fusion for Qwen3-30B. At compile time, we swap the aforementioned fusion pattern & fuse with aiter's high-performance HIP kernel `fused_qk_norm_rope_cache_pts_quant_shuffle ` for all decoder layers that satisfy a preset token compile range guarded by `rope_kvcache_fusion_max_token_num`. 
- This kernel supports writing KV Cache in both shuffle and non-shuffle layouts. ROCM_AITER_FA and ROCM_AITER_UNIFIED_ATTN require the fused kernel to write in non-shuffle mode, as this corresponds to the flash layout expected by FA variants. 
- As for ROCM_ATTN, modifications were needed on both the prefill and decode path kernels (prefill Triton, decode Triton 2D for non-power-of-2 block sizes, and decode paged attention ll4 GFX9 variants) so that they can read the value cache in the interleaved mode, which the fused kernel writes in when shuffle=True. The key cache read logic is untouched as the fused kernel already writes in the correct format, which the prefill and decode paths expect.
- When shuffle=True, the fused kernel writes K with shape (blocks, heads, dim/x, block_size, x) and V with shape (blocks, heads, block_size/x, dim, x). Decode paths already natively read K in the same format, but it currently hardcodes reading of the V cache with an expected shape of (blocks, heads, dims, block_size).

## Test Plan

To test this fusion out, please pass the attached toggle through the pass config under the `--compilation-config` when launching with `vllm serve`:

`'pass_config': {
        'fuse_qk_norm_rope_kvcache': True,
        'rope_kvcache_fusion_max_token_num': 256
}`

For example, something like this:

`VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER=1 \
vllm serve /path-to-your/Qwen3-30B-A3B \
  --host 0.0.0.0 \
  --port 8080 \
  --attention-backend ROCM_ATTN \
  --no-enable-prefix-caching \
  -O1 \
  --compilation-config '{
    "pass_config": {
      "fuse_qk_norm_rope_kvcache": true,
      "rope_kvcache_fusion_ma

prs.title: [Kernel][Helion] Fix inductor fusion of Helion HOP
prs.author: gmagogsfm
prs.body_head:
This PR fixes following error when compiling Helion kernel with TorchInductor fusion enabled.

Previously, we manually added kernels to the side table and imported low level modules — but missed importing helion._compiler._inductor.template_buffer, which has the side effect of registering inductor lowerings for the Helion HOPs. 

Causing this failure:
```
AssertionError: helion_kernel_wrapper_functional is not an OpOverload
```

This PR now uses a more broad import and avoid fiddling with Helion internals to fix the issue. 

Test Plan:
Added a dedicated unit test to cover torch.compile + inductor fusion + helion kernel. Unfortunately this test won't be run in CI until PyTorch version is bumped to 2.12 though.

prs.title: [Mistral Grammar] Fix tool and reasoning parsing
prs.author: juliendenize
prs.body_head:
## Purpose
When Mistral models are served with `--tool-call-parser mistral` and a `mistral-common` compatible tokenizer (tekken/v11+), #38150 introduced grammar-based tool-call enforcement: `adjust_request` injects a Lark grammar from mistral-common's grammar factory into `structured_outputs`, constraining model output to valid Mistral tool-call formatting at the decoding level.

However, that PR only handled grammar injection. The serving layer still fell through to generic vLLM tool-call parsing paths that don't understand Mistral's grammar-constrained format. This broke tool-call extraction for all tool_choice modes (auto, required, named, none), both streaming and non-streaming.

This PR makes the serving layer recognize grammar-constrained Mistral output and route it through MistralToolParser for correct parsing, rather than falling through to the generic paths. It also ensures `tool_choice="none"` still calls adjust_request on grammar-capable tokenizers so the grammar factory can suppress special-token leakage (e.g., [TOOL_CALLS] appearing in plain text output).

This is the second PR to improve https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/pull/37081 first attempt.

## Test Plan
The branch adds:
- Unit tests in tests/tool_parsers/test_mistral_tool_parser.py
- E2E tests in tests/tool_use/mistral/test_mistral_tool_calls.py

```bash
# Unit tests
pytest tests/tool_parsers/test_mistral_tool_parser.py -v
# E2E tests
pytest tests/tool_use/mistral/test_mistral_tool_calls.py -v
```

prs.title: [compile] Invoke split FX graph by codegen.
prs.author: zhxchen17
prs.body_head:
Summary:

This PR reduces inference loop runtime overhead by codegen-ing slightly faster Python
code instead of invoking the FX graph directly after compilation.

Context:

Today VllmBackend returns a callable as a FX GraphModule with multiple submodules with the following code:
```
def forward(self, ...):
    self.submod_0(...)
    self.submod_1(...)
    ...
```
FX graph execution has some overhead due to:
1. getattr() calls to fetch submodules.
2. submodule calls will push multiple levels of CPython stack frame before getting to the real kernels.

We address this by introducing a new codegen layer after all compiler passes and right
before inference runtime. In this codegen layer we get full customizability over how the graph is executed.

Sample generated code:
```
    submod_0 = _submods[0](l_input_ids_, s72, l_self_modules_embed_tokens_parameters_weight_, l_self_modules_layers_modules_0_modules_input_layernorm_parameters_weight_, l_self_modules_layers_modules_0_modules_self_attn_modules_qkv_proj_parameters_weight_, l_positions_, l_self_modules_layers_modules_0_modules_self_attn_modules_rotary_emb_buffers_cos_sin_cache_)
    getitem = submod_0[0]
    getitem_1 = submod_0[1]
    getitem_2 = submod_0[2]
    getitem_3 = submod_0[3]
    getitem_4 = submod_0[4]
    submod_1 = _submods[1](getitem, s72, getitem_1, getitem_2, getitem_3)
    submod_2 = _submods[2](getitem_3, s72, l_self_modules_layers_modules_0_modules_self_attn_modules_o_proj_parameters_weight_, l_self_modules_layers_modules_0_modules_post_attention_layernorm_parameters_weight_, getitem_4, l_self_modules_layers_modules_0_modules_mlp_modules_gate_up_proj_parameters_weight_, l_self_modules_layers_modules_0_modules_mlp_modules_down_proj_parameters_weight_, l_self_modules_layers_modules_1_modules_input_layernorm_parameters_weight_, l_self_modules_layers_modules_1_modules_self_attn_modules_qkv_proj_parameters_weight_, l_positions_, l_self_modules_layers_modules_0_modules_self_at

prs.title: [ROCm] Enable FP8 KV-cache and relax constraints for RDNA4 custom paged attention
prs.author: laudney
prs.body_head:
## Summary

Enable FP8 KV-cache support for the gfx12 (RDNA4) custom paged attention kernel via software dequantization, and fix several overly-restrictive constraints that prevented the Navi kernel from being used in valid configurations.

**FP8 KV-cache halves the KV-cache memory footprint** (16-bit → 8-bit per element), roughly doubling token capacity. On an AMD Radeon AI PRO R9700 (32GB), this increases KV-cache from ~132K tokens to ~264K tokens for a Qwen3-Coder-30B AWQ model — the difference between 1.4x and 2.75x concurrent 96K-context requests.

### Kernel changes (`csrc/rocm/attention.cu`)

- **`convert_b8x8_to_b16x8()`**: Portable FP8 → FP16/BF16 vector conversion using HIP runtime `fp8::vec_conversion` (works across gfx11/gfx12 without arch-specific intrinsics)
- **FP8 dequant wired into gfx12 QKV kernel**: When `kv_cache_dtype == "fp8"`, V-cache loads go through software dequant using per-block KV cache scale factors before the dot-product accumulation
- **GQA ratio 1–2 support on gfx12**: The gfx12 kernel correctly handles GQA ratios down to 1 (MHA), but the Python guard restricted to `gqa_ratio >= 3` — now `gqa_ratio >= 1`

### Platform guard fixes (`vllm/platforms/rocm.py`)

| Guard | Before (bug) | After (fix) |
|-------|-------------|-------------|
| `_ON_GFX12` | didn't exist | New flag to distinguish gfx12 (FP8-capable) from gfx11 (no FP8) |
| `block_size` | `== 16` only | `== 16` on gfx11, `== 16 or == 32` on gfx12 (VBLOCKS_PER_LANE=1) |
| `head_size` | `64 or 128` on Navi | `128` only — kernel computes `vhead_elem = warpid * 16 + lane16id` without bounds check, reads past head slice for 64-wide heads |
| `kv_cache_dtype` | `"auto"` only | `"auto"` or `"fp8"/"fp8_e4m3"` on gfx12 |

### Test changes (`tests/kernels/attention/test_attention.py`)

- FP8 KV-cache tests now run on `supports_fp8()` platforms instead of blanket-skipping all FP8 on Navi

### Related PRs (RDNA4/gfx12 series)
- #34709 — [ROCm] Enable wvSplitK/wvSplitKQ skinny GEMM kernels

prs.title: refactor hard coded device string in test files under tests/compile tests/quantization tests/models and tests/model_executor
prs.author: wincent8
prs.body_head:
This PR replaces hardcoded "cuda" device strings with dynamic platform checks across tests/compile, tests/quantization, tests/models, tests/model_executor and tests/basic_correctness. By utilizing current_platform.device_type, we enable these test suites to be reused across different hardware accelerators (e.g., ROCm, Gaudi, XPU) without manual modification.
Currently, many tests in the V1 engine and LoRA modules are coupled specifically to CUDA. This makes it difficult to verify feature parity on non-NVIDIA hardware. This PR generalizes the device handling to ensure that "cuda-centric" code becomes "accelerator-agnostic."
Proposed Changes
I have implemented the following systematic replacements:

use DEVICE_TYPE (inferred from current_platform.device_type) replace hardcode cuda
use DEVICES ([f"{DEVICE_TYPE}:{i}" for i in range(1 if current_platform.device_count() == 1 else 2)]) replace CUDA_DEVICES ([f"cuda:{i} for i in range(1 if current_platform.device_count() == 1 else 2)"]
Impact

Test Parity: Allows non-CUDA CI pipelines to run the exact same validation logic used for NVIDIA GPUs.

prs.title: [ROCm] Align AiterFlashAttentionImpl attn_type check with backend
prs.author: Bortlesboat
prs.body_head:
`AiterFlashAttentionBackend.supports_attn_type()` correctly rejects `ENCODER_DECODER` with a detailed comment about why cross-attention would produce wrong results (cu_seqlens_k set to decoder query_start_loc + causal=True).

But `AiterFlashAttentionImpl.__init__` accepts both `DECODER` and `ENCODER_DECODER`. If ENCODER_DECODER were ever passed through (e.g., via direct instantiation or a future code path), the impl would silently produce incorrect attention outputs instead of raising.

This aligns the impl with the backend by only accepting `DECODER`, and improves the error message to explain the technical reason.

No duplicate PRs found. AI assistance was used for code search; all changes reviewed by human submitter.

prs.title: Bugfix: `use_existing_torch.py`: Glob recursive subdirs in requirements (fixes #39024)
prs.author: netanel-haber
prs.body_head:
#39024 added nesting to the requirements folder. `use_existing_torch.py` expected all requirements files to be flat. This pr adds recursion to the glob so `use_existing_torch` can work properly again. 

Repro:

1. Run the script on current `main`: `python use_existing_torch.py`. 
2. Observe: only top-level `requirements/*.txt` / `*.in` are modified. Nested files (e.g. `requirements/build/*`, `requirements/test/*`) are untouched.
3. Apply recursive glob change:
   ```diff
   - glob("requirements/*.txt")
   - glob("requirements/*.in")
   + glob("requirements/**/*.txt", recursive=True)
   + glob("requirements/**/*.in", recursive=True)
   ```
4. Observe:
   - Torch dependencies are removed across all requirement files:
     - `requirements/build/*`
     - `requirements/test/*`
     - `cuda`, `rocm`, `xpu` variants
   - Behavior is now consistent with pre-restructure layout.

prs.title: [Bugfix] Fix `vllm bench serve` to count multimodal tokens in "total input tokens"
prs.author: mgehre-amd
prs.body_head:
## Purpose

When benchmarking multimodal models, `vllm bench serve` reports `total_input_tokens` based on the client-side text-only prompt length, excluding image/encoder tokens. The server already reports the correct count (text + image tokens) via `usage.prompt_tokens` in the streaming response, but it was not captured.

Capture `prompt_tokens` from the streaming usage chunk and use it for input token metrics so `total_input_tokens` reflects the actual prefill size.

## Test Plan
```
vllm bench serve --model Qwen/Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct --port 8000 --random-input-len 512 --output-len 128 --num-prompts 1  --backend openai-chat --endpoint /v1/chat/completions --dataset-name
   random-mm --random-mm-base-items-per-request 1 --random-mm-limit-mm-per-prompt '{"image": 1, "video": 0}' --random-mm-bucket-config '{(1024, 800, 1): 1.0}
```

Before:
```
  Total input tokens:                      512
```
After:
```
  Total input tokens:                      1606
```

prs.title: feat: add TxtSlicesDataset to allow sampling slices from txt file for benchmarking
prs.author: jdebache
prs.body_head:
## Purpose

Sampling randomly directly from a tokenizer for benchmarking creates data that is not ideal to benchmark when using speculative decoding or expert parallelism.

On the other hand, random datasets are very flexible and offer complete control on the input and output sequence lengths, which is desirable to create reproducible benchmarks.

This PR introduces a new type of benchmarking dataset called `TxtSlicesDataset` which offers a compromise between the flexibility of a random dataset and the fidelity of a real dataset. It allows sampling slices from a user-provided txt file.

## Content
- The implementation of `TxtSlicesDataset`
- Fixes to typing in `datasets.py`
- A unit test for the new dataset type

prs.title: [CPU] Replace OMP initialization
prs.author: kot-begemot-uk
prs.body_head:
OMP initializes on-load based on environment variables. Once it has initialized changing the environment has no effect.

Additionally, using POSIX set/get affinity calls from inside an OMP thread is ill advised at best. It may and does cause vllm to hang later in some configurations. It is also pointless - there are OMP standard directives like OMP_PLACES and OMP_PROC_BIND which perform the same function in a way which is specified in the standard and is guaranteed to work with all implementations.

<!-- markdownlint-disable -->

## Purpose
OMP initializes on-library-load based on environment variables set at that time. Once it has initialized, changing the environment has no effect. Hence, some of the CPU initialization code used at present has no effect.

Additionally, using POSIX set/get affinity calls from inside an OMP thread is ill advised at best. It may and does cause vllm to hang later in some configurations - bug #32651

It is also pointless - there are OMP standard directives like OMP_PLACES and OMP_PROC_BIND which perform the same function in a way which is specified in the standard and is guaranteed to work with all implementations. It is also future proof - enhancements to the libraries and/or using different libraries will not change the behaviour. 

## Test Plan

## Test Result

---
<details>
<summary>Replace AdHoc OMP initialization with an OMP spec compliant one </summary>

- [ ] Fix https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/issues/32651 and other OMP initialization and performance issues
- [ ] Does not need any specific CPU test suite changes. 
- [ ] No soft hangs without libiomp5 preload on machines with more than 16 cores.
- [ ] No documentation changes - backwards compatible. 
</details>
